title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: Started from 24 raw findings across four personas and collapsed them to 14. The high-confidence core is three-way agreement on the PCI-grade card-data leak (console.log of the raw charge body) and the empty money-path catch block, plus two-way agreement on the always-zero discount_amount bug, the unvalidated couponCode, the second debug console.log, the `as any` response, and the doStuff/tmp naming. I kept two single-persona must-fix correctness items the others missed (negative charge from unbounded fixed discount, no floor) and the missing tests. I downgraded kiss_zealot's over-engineering finding from must to should (real but not a merge blocker) and merged its abstract-class-vs-interface point into it. Pruned nothing as outright false — every claim checks out against the diff — but merged heavily where personas described the same lines from different angles. Severity calls: card-in-logs, swallowed catch, zero-discount bug, and negative-amount are MUST; everything else is should/suggestion.

findings.title: console.log dumps full charge body (PAN/CVC) to stdout — PCI violation
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serializes the entire raw request body — including card.number and card.cvc — to stdout before any parsing or redaction. This bypasses the project's pino logger and its redaction allowlist; card data flows to wherever stdout goes (log aggregators, CloudWatch, Splunk). PCI-DSS forbids writing CVC to any log sink. Remove this line entirely. If request tracing is needed, log only non-sensitive identifiers (customerId, amount, currency) through pino after the schema parse.

findings.title: Empty catch swallows coupon errors — charge silently proceeds at wrong amount
findings.message: `catch (e) { // TODO }` discards every exception from `doStuff`. On any throw, `finalAmount` keeps its pre-throw value, `payload.amount` was already mutated to match, the charge fires anyway, and the caller gets a 201 with `discount_amount: 0` and no log trail. Swallowing exceptions on a money path violates fail-closed. Either handle it meaningfully (log via pino + return a 422 for an unresolvable coupon) or let it propagate to the outer handler. An empty TODO catch must not ship on a payment path.

findings.title: discount_amount is always 0 — amount overwritten before the subtraction
findings.message: Line 24 does `payload.amount = finalAmount`, so when line 37 computes `payload.amount - finalAmount` both operands hold the discounted value and the result is always 0. The mutation is also unnecessary — `finalAmount` already holds the discounted value passed to `charge`. Fix: capture the original first — `const originalAmount = payload.amount; ... response.discount_amount = originalAmount - finalAmount;` — and drop the mutation.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy can return a negative amount — no lower-bound guard
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt` has no floor. A 300-cent charge with FIVEOFF (500) returns -200, and because the catch above swallows everything, that negative amount is passed straight to the gateway. Add `Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)` at minimum, or throw a domain error (e.g. DiscountExceedsAmountError) so the caller can return a 422.

findings.title: couponCode read from raw body, bypasses Zod, reflected back unvalidated
findings.message: `couponCode` is plucked from `req.body` before `chargeSchema.parse` runs and is echoed back verbatim in `response.coupon_code`. The Zod boundary never sees it — no length cap, no charset constraint — so an arbitrarily long or crafted string reaches the lookup, the logs, and the client. Add `couponCode: z.string().regex(/^[A-Z0-9]{1,20}$/).optional()` to `chargeSchema` and read it from the validated `payload`, never from the raw body.

findings.title: Unknown coupon code logged via console.log — log-injection + bypasses pino
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` writes a raw, user-supplied string to stdout. With newlines or ANSI/JSON fragments an attacker can poison or spoof log entries, and it bypasses the structured, redacted pino logger. Replace with `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon')` and enforce the charset/length cap in chargeSchema so an arbitrary string never reaches the sink.

findings.title: `const response: any = transaction` — untyped passthrough of full transaction to client
findings.message: Casting the transaction to `any` and attaching snake_case fields surrenders all compiler knowledge of the object and ships every field `payments.charge` returns straight to the API caller — potentially gateway tokens or other internal fields that should never cross the boundary. Define an explicit response type and construct it field-by-field (transactionId, amount, currency, discountAmount, couponCode) so the serialization surface is known and intentional.

findings.title: Strategy + Factory + abstract class for a 6-row lookup table
findings.message: Three classes, an inheritance tree, and a factory dispatch six hard-coded discount rules. The machinery adds zero capability over a plain record plus one function and obscures the logic (the always-zero bug above hid inside it). Note also that `AbstractCouponStrategy` has no shared concrete behavior — it's an interface in disguise forcing single inheritance. Replace the whole file with a `Record<string, {kind, value}>` lookup and one `applyCoupon(amount, code)` function; reintroduce abstractions when date-bound/customer-specific coupons actually land and their shape is known.

findings.title: Exported `doStuff` plus `tmp`/`result2`/`x`/`p`/`amt` — meaningless names
findings.message: `doStuff` is the public API the controller imports and tells readers nothing. Internals use `tmp`, `result2`, `x`, `p`, `amt`. Rename the function to `applyCouponDiscount`, `tmp` to `strategy`/`handler`, inline `result2` into a direct return, and give the strategy fields meaningful names (`percentOff`, `amountOff`).

findings.title: Response keys discount_amount / coupon_code break camelCase API contract
findings.message: The rest of the charge response uses camelCase (transactionId, customerId). Adding `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` forces every consumer to special-case this endpoint. Rename to `discountAmount` and `couponCode` to match house style.

findings.title: `var` and `==` throughout — convention drift, likely lint failures
findings.message: `var x` (l16), `var tmp` (l58), and the `==` comparisons in `CouponHandlerFactory.build` (l34–45) and `if (tmp == null)` (l59) violate the codebase's const/let and `===` conventions and presumably the ESLint config (prefer-const, eqeqeq). Switch to const/let and `===`; if the linter doesn't already catch these, tighten it.

findings.title: Zero tests for new money-math and coupon dispatch
findings.message: Tests were deferred to a follow-up, but this is money-adjacent branching logic and the follow-up rarely happens. Untested surface includes percentage truncation/rounding, fixed-discount math (the negative-amount case is already broken), all six codes dispatching correctly, the unknown-code fallthrough, and the empty/undefined couponCode early return. Ship unit tests for the discount math and dispatch with this PR, not after.

findings.title: `theData` is a meaningless name for the raw request body
findings.message: `const theData = req.body` communicates nothing and reads like a placeholder that escaped review. If you want a name contrasting the unvalidated input with the validated `payload`, call it `rawBody`; otherwise use `req.body` directly.

findings.title: Commented-out FREESHIP branch — delete it, git remembers
findings.message: The dead FREESHIP branch makes every future reader stop to decide whether it's a plan, a rollback, or a fossil. Version history is the backup; delete it.
